scenario: A real-time trading dashboard with 200+ price tickers updating 10x/sec. Team is choosing between Preact Signals (via @preact/signals-react), React state with useSyncExternalStore + selectors, or Jotai atoms. The current React 19 implementation causes the entire ticker grid to re-render on every WebSocket message.
context: Team of 6 engineers, 14-month-old codebase, profiling shows 40ms render cost per WS frame at p95, target is sub-10ms. Mostly senior devs but two juniors recently joined.
alex_initial_position: Signals. The mental model is wrong with React for this. We need fine-grained reactivity at the DOM level, not VDOM diffing. Every other framework solved this; we're paying a tax for React's design.
sam_initial_position: useSyncExternalStore with carefully scoped selectors. Signals are an escape hatch that bifurcates the codebase. Two juniors will write half-signal, half-state code and we'll regret it in six months.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: I profiled this. Even with React.memo on every ticker cell, we burn 28ms in reconciliation because the parent re-renders trigger memo checks for 200 children. That's wasted work no matter how tight the children are.
debate.evidence: React Profiler trace shows 200 memo-bailouts at 0.14ms each = 28ms just to decide NOT to render.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You're describing a parent-re-render problem, not a React problem. Move the ticker subscriptions into each cell with useSyncExternalStore selecting only its symbol's price. The parent never re-renders on price changes.
debate.evidence: useSyncExternalStore with a per-symbol selector bails out at the hook level before reconciliation begins for that subtree.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Fine, but now I have 200 store subscriptions, each running a selector on every WS message. That's 200 * messages/sec function calls. Signals give us O(1) update propagation - the cell that owns priceSignal['AAPL'] updates, period.
debate.evidence: Signal graph traversal updates only dependents; useSyncExternalStore selectors all run on every store notification regardless of whether their slice changed.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You can shard the store - one store per symbol, or notify with a symbol key and have selectors short-circuit. The selector cost is nanoseconds. The real cost is teaching the team two reactivity models and debugging which one fired.
debate.evidence: Linear's eng blog: they removed signals after onboarding cost spiked - PRs took 2x longer to review due to dual mental models.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: I'll concede the dual-model cost is real. But here's the test: can a junior add a new derived value (like 'top 10 movers')? With signals it's `computed(() => ...)`. With useSyncExternalStore they need to understand snapshot stability, ref equality, and useMemo.
debate.evidence: In our last hire's first PR, they returned a fresh array from a selector and caused an infinite render loop. Signals' computed has no such footgun.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's a tooling problem solved by a custom hook: `useStoreSelector(key, deriver)` with built-in shallow equality. Wrap the footgun, expose the safe API. We get React's ecosystem (devtools, Suspense, transitions) and a junior-safe API.
debate.evidence: Zustand's `useShallow` did exactly this; their GitHub issues for 'infinite loop' dropped 80% after adding it.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK - here's my real concern with signals: React 19 concurrent rendering. Signals mutate during render in some patterns, which breaks tearing guarantees. If we use startTransition for the symbol filter, signals can show inconsistent prices mid-transition.
debate.evidence: Preact signals' README explicitly notes that concurrent mode tearing requires their useSignals() Babel transform; without it, you get visual inconsistencies.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's the kill shot. We're committed to concurrent features for the filter UX. Let's go useSyncExternalStore with a sharded store keyed by symbol, plus a `useTickerPrice(symbol)` hook that hides the selector boilerplate.
debate.evidence: useSyncExternalStore is the official tearing-safe primitive; it was literally added for this use case.

where_they_agree: The current giant-Context approach is broken and must change
Per-cell subscription is the right granularity
A junior-safe wrapper hook is required regardless of underlying primitive
Profiling drove the decision, not theory
where_they_disagree: Whether signals' DX benefits outweigh the dual-paradigm cost
Whether selector-cost or render-cost dominates at scale
How much to weight concurrent rendering compatibility
context_dependent_answer: Choose useSyncExternalStore with a sharded store when (a) you use React 19 concurrent features, (b) team has React-only experience, or (c) the app must integrate with Suspense. Choose Signals when (a) you're building a fully isolated widget with no concurrent features, (b) the team has Vue/Solid background, or (c) update frequency exceeds 1000/sec where even selector overhead matters.

strengths: Per-symbol updates do not touch other cells
weaknesses: Slightly higher overhead than signals at extreme update rates
Requires Babel transform for concurrent safety
Bifurcates state model in the codebase
Devtools story weaker than React DevTools

decision_framework: Ask: (1) Are we using React 19 concurrent features (transitions, Suspense for data)? If yes -> useSyncExternalStore. (2) Is the team React-native or polyglot? React-only -> useSyncExternalStore. (3) Is update rate > 1kHz per cell? If yes, consider signals despite costs. (4) Are we shipping a widget or owning the whole app? Widget -> signals OK; whole app -> stay native React.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think the problem is 'React is slow' and reach for a different library. Seniors recognize the problem is 'we put state at the wrong level' - moving subscriptions down the tree fixes 90% of perf issues without changing libraries. They also miss that concurrent rendering imposes correctness constraints (tearing) that some 'faster' libraries silently violate.

scenario: A Next.js 15 e-commerce site is deciding where to draw the Server Component / Client Component boundary. Specifically: the product detail page has a price block (server-rendered, SEO), variant selector (interactive), recommended products carousel (interactive but data-heavy), and reviews (paginated, can be either).
context: Team of 10, migrating from Pages Router. Lighthouse score is a board-level metric. Conversion rate dropped 3% after a previous over-eager 'use client' migration.
alex_initial_position: Push the boundary as deep as possible. Every 'use client' is a JS bundle cost. The carousel and reviews should be Server Components with client islands only for the interactive bits (next/prev buttons, expand button).
sam_initial_position: Draw the boundary at the feature level, not the leaf level. Trying to interleave server and client components inside a single feature creates a prop-passing nightmare and breaks colocation. Reviews and carousel become client components in full.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: The carousel has 12 product cards. Each card is 80% static (image, name, price) and 20% interactive (add-to-cart button). If we make the whole card client, we ship the entire product card markup as JS. That's roughly 4KB per card * 12 = 48KB of avoidable JS.
debate.evidence: Bundle analyzer on our staging deploy: PCard.client.tsx contributes 47KB gzipped to /product/[slug] route.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You're optimizing bytes and ignoring complexity. To split a card you need: Server card component, Client wrapper for the button, prop drilling for productId and variant. Now changing the card layout means touching two files and thinking about serialization. Engineer-hours cost more than 48KB.
debate.evidence: Stripe's design system docs explicitly recommend 'feature-level client boundary' for exactly this reason - they tried leaf-level and reverted after 6 months.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: 48KB is 200ms of parse time on a mid-tier Android. On a product page where the user is deciding to buy. That's not bytes vs hours, that's conversion vs hours. Show me a feature flag rollback where conversion went up after adding 48KB of JS.
debate.evidence: Akamai study: 100ms latency = 7% conversion drop. Our last bundle increase of 60KB correlated with a 2% drop in add-to-cart rate.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: OK that's a real number. But the leaf-level pattern works for static-heavy components like the card. Where it breaks is the variant selector - it needs server data (available variants) AND interactive state (selected variant) AND it triggers a server action (add to cart). Splitting that is a maze.
debate.evidence: Vercel's Next.js docs example for variant selectors shows them as full client components with initial data passed as props.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Agreed on the variant selector - that's inherently client. My rule: a component is client if its primary purpose is interaction. Cards are primarily display with a button - that button can be a 200-byte client component. The variant selector is primarily interactive.
debate.evidence: The 'islands' architecture popularized by Astro: 90% of pages are static HTML, with small JS islands for the parts that need it.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Fine. I'll concede leaf-level for cards. But reviews are different - pagination, sorting, expand/collapse, helpful votes. Every interaction. Making that 'mostly server with client controls' means re-fetching server components on every sort click via router.refresh(). That's a worse UX than client-side state.
debate.evidence: router.refresh() in Next 15 still re-renders the full RSC tree from the root - measured 400ms on our review section.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Use a Server Action that returns just the review HTML, mount it via a client component that calls the action. Or use Suspense streaming for the initial paginated load and a client component only for the sort/filter controls that calls server actions for new pages.
debate.evidence: Next 15 supports 'use server' functions returning JSX since v15.1 - we can return a Server Component subtree from an action.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That works for sort but not for optimistic 'helpful' votes - those need useOptimistic which is client-only. So the review item itself is client. Now we have: server review list, client review item, server action for sort. That's three layers for one feature. Per-feature is simpler: reviews is one client component that calls the API.
debate.evidence: useOptimistic and useFormState are only available in client components per React 19 docs.

where_they_agree: Variant selector must be a client component
Inherently interactive primary-purpose components belong on the client
Bundle size on commerce pages directly impacts conversion
'use client' should never be at the page root
where_they_disagree: Whether to push boundary to leaf level or feature level by default
How much complexity cost to pay for bundle savings
Whether server-action JSX returns justify the split
context_dependent_answer: Use leaf-level boundaries when: (a) the component is display-heavy with small interactive bits (product cards, nav items), (b) bundle size is on a critical conversion path, (c) the team is comfortable with RSC patterns. Use feature-level boundaries when: (a) the feature has dense interactivity (reviews with optimistic updates, dashboards), (b) the team is mid-migration and RSC mental model is still being built, (c) the feature uses useOptimistic, useFormState, or other client-only React 19 APIs.

strengths: Minimal client JS (~600 bytes per card)
weaknesses: Refactoring card layout touches both files
Full review markup ships as JS (~8KB)
Initial render not streamable past hydration
SEO crawlers see initial reviews only

decision_framework: For each component ask: (1) Is its primary purpose interaction or display? Display -> server, interaction -> client. (2) Does it use client-only React 19 APIs (useOptimistic, useFormState, useEffect)? Yes -> client. (3) Is it on a conversion-critical bundle path? Yes -> push boundary as deep as possible. (4) Will splitting create more than 3 files for one feature? Yes -> hoist boundary up one level.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors either sprinkle 'use client' everywhere (defeating RSC) or try to make everything a Server Component (breaking interactivity). Seniors understand that the boundary is a design decision per feature, weighing bundle cost against cognitive cost. They also know that 'use client' is a network boundary - props must serialize - and design APIs around that constraint upfront instead of fighting it later.

scenario: A SaaS analytics dashboard loads 8 widgets per page, each calling a different API endpoint. Team debates Suspense with React Query + ErrorBoundary versus traditional isLoading/isError flags in each widget. The product team wants 'no loading spinners' - widgets should fade in as they load with a smooth waterfall feel.
context: Team of 12, React 19 + TanStack Query v5. Some widgets take 200ms (cached), some 3s (cold compute). Users frequently refresh and re-filter. Current implementation uses isLoading flags and ships skeleton screens.
alex_initial_position: Keep isLoading/isError flags. Suspense looks elegant in demos but breaks the moment you need granular control - retry buttons, partial data display, conditional skeletons. The 'no spinners' goal is achievable with regular state.
sam_initial_position: Suspense throughout. The waterfall coordination, the SuspenseList ordering, the cleaner component code - all of it is what Suspense was built for. We're paying the complexity tax of useQuery for no benefit when we could just write the data path.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Look at a typical widget today: useQuery returns data/isLoading/isError, then we have three branches in JSX. With Suspense, the widget assumes data exists. The loading and error states move to declarative boundaries above. Code drops 30% in size.
debate.evidence: I refactored RevenueWidget last week with useSuspenseQuery - went from 87 lines to 54 lines, mostly removing isLoading branches.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: You moved complexity, not removed it. The parent now has to know which children Suspend and arrange ErrorBoundary + Suspense pairs accordingly. When a PM asks 'why does this widget freeze when its sibling errors' you'll dig through three nesting levels of boundaries.
debate.evidence: Tweet from Tanner Linsley (TanStack author): 'useSuspenseQuery is great until you need to debug error propagation across boundaries.'

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's a one-time setup cost in a layout component, not a per-widget cost. Once we have WidgetShell that wraps Suspense + ErrorBoundary, widgets are plug-and-play. New widgets don't think about loading/error - they just use the data.
debate.evidence: Vercel dashboard ships this pattern - their Card primitive wraps Suspense and an error boundary, widgets are pure render functions.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK fair, but what about retry? With useQuery I get refetch() as a function. With useSuspenseQuery, retry means re-rendering the ErrorBoundary's children via reset. The retry button lives outside the widget that knows what failed. That's worse DX.
debate.evidence: The QueryErrorResetBoundary docs explicitly note this awkwardness and the reset() pattern requires coordination across components.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: react-error-boundary's resetKeys solves that - pass the query key as a reset key, the boundary handles retry without coordination. Widget stays pure. We get the 'no spinners' UX for free because Suspense fades in via React 19's improved transitions.
debate.evidence: react-error-boundary 4.x + React 19 startTransition: rendering a new query key with startTransition keeps the old UI visible until new data is ready.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Here's my real objection: waterfall. If I have 8 widgets each in their own Suspense, they fire sequentially because React renders top-down and each Suspends in turn. With useQuery they fire in parallel because all hooks run before any data is needed.
debate.evidence: React docs explicitly warn about this: 'Avoid Suspense waterfalls by prefetching.' Our 8 widgets would go from 3s total (parallel) to 8s total (waterfall).

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's solved by prefetchQuery in the parent or a route loader. We call queryClient.prefetchQuery for all 8 in the loader, then each useSuspenseQuery picks up the cached promise. Parallel fire, suspense rendering. Best of both.
debate.evidence: TanStack Query v5 docs section 'Suspense + Prefetch': prefetch fires all queries in parallel, useSuspenseQuery reads from the in-flight cache.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That works, but now we have a hidden coupling: the route loader must know every query every widget will run. Add a widget, remember to add a prefetch, or you break the perf model silently. That's exactly the kind of action-at-a-distance I hate.
debate.evidence: We had a regression last month where someone added a widget without prefetching - p95 page load went from 1.2s to 4.1s and nobody noticed for 2 weeks.

where_they_agree: Skeleton-then-content (no spinners) is the right UX goal
ErrorBoundary should wrap groups of widgets, not be omitted
React Query is the right cache layer regardless of read pattern
The decision affects how new widgets are added more than existing ones
where_they_disagree: Whether implicit Suspense > explicit loading flags
How to handle retry and reset ergonomically
Whether waterfall prevention via prefetch is worth the coupling cost
context_dependent_answer: Use Suspense + useSuspenseQuery when: (a) widgets are uniform and added rarely, (b) route loaders or RSC handle prefetching deterministically, (c) the team has internalized boundary placement. Use useQuery with isLoading flags when: (a) widgets vary widely in lifecycle (some need polling, some need retry, some need partial data), (b) the team is mid-migration to React 19, (c) widgets are added frequently by less-senior engineers.
decision_framework: Choose Suspense when: route loaders or RSC handle prefetch, widgets are stable, team has 6+ months React 19 experience. Choose explicit flags when: widgets vary in fetch lifecycle, juniors add widgets often, you need fine-grained retry/partial-data. Hybrid is valid: Suspense for the dashboard shell, explicit flags inside individual widgets for sub-fetches.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think Suspense magically parallelizes fetches - it doesn't. It serializes them top-down unless you prefetch. They also underestimate the boundary placement cost: a single misplaced Suspense can collapse 8 widgets into one loading state and lose the 'progressive reveal' UX. Seniors design the boundary topology before writing the first widget.

scenario: A design system team must choose between Tailwind v4 (CSS-first config, @theme variables) for the new component library versus CSS Modules with design tokens via CSS custom properties. The library will be consumed by 6 internal apps with diverging needs (one needs heavy theming, others use defaults).
context: Team of 5 on the DS team, 60 engineers consuming. Existing v3 Tailwind in some apps, plain CSS in others. Theming requirements: per-tenant brand colors at runtime, not build time.
alex_initial_position: CSS Modules + custom properties. Runtime theming is the requirement. Tailwind's dark: variant pattern hardcodes contexts at build time. Custom properties cascade naturally, runtime theme switching is a class swap on <html>.
sam_initial_position: Tailwind v4. The @theme + CSS variable architecture in v4 was designed exactly for this. The new @theme directive defines tokens that compile to CSS variables, so runtime swap works. Plus we get the consistency win across 60 engineers.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Tailwind's runtime theming story is still bolted on. v4 emits CSS variables for tokens, sure, but utility names like bg-primary-500 resolve at build time. If a tenant adds a new color we can't generate a class for it without a rebuild.
debate.evidence: Tailwind v4 docs note: arbitrary value classes like bg-[--my-color] work, but named utilities require build-time registration.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's actually the point - tenants don't add arbitrary colors. They override the 12 brand tokens. We define --color-brand-primary once in @theme, tenants swap its value at runtime. Utilities like bg-brand-primary automatically pick up the new value because they compile to var(--color-brand-primary).
debate.evidence: Tailwind v4 changelog: '@theme variables are exposed as CSS custom properties and can be overridden at runtime without rebuilds.'

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK that's fair for color. But what about spacing, radius, font-size - things tenants might want different? With CSS Modules I write .button { padding: var(--space-button-padding); } and any token works the same way. Tailwind requires every token to be in @theme.
debate.evidence: Real example: client wanted a 'spacious' tenant variant with 1.5x padding. We'd need parallel padding utilities or arbitrary value escapes everywhere.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Scale of arbitrary spacing variants is bounded - density modes (compact/comfortable/spacious) are 3 values, not infinite. Define --space-density as a multiplier. The 60-engineer consistency win outweighs perfect flexibility.
debate.evidence: Vercel and Linear both run multi-tenant theming on Tailwind via CSS variable density modes.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Consistency is real, but so is lock-in. If we ship our DS as Tailwind, every consumer must adopt Tailwind too. We have 2 apps on plain CSS - they'd need Tailwind just to use our Button. CSS Modules exports a className that works anywhere.
debate.evidence: @tailwindcss/vite adds 180KB to dev bundle and a build step. Two apps are static-html embeds where this is a no-go.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Strong point. But our DS ships compiled CSS, not Tailwind source. Consumers get a CSS file with already-resolved utilities and our component CSS - no Tailwind install. Tailwind is our build tool, not their dependency.
debate.evidence: shadcn-ui and Radix Themes both ship this way - the consumer never sees Tailwind classes from us, only resolved CSS.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: If we ship compiled CSS anyway, the question becomes 'which authoring experience for the DS team itself?' That's 5 people. CSS Modules give us scoped class names, native features (container queries, :has(), color-mix()), and zero JS toolchain.
debate.evidence: All the new CSS features land in browsers months before Tailwind exposes utilities. We waited 6 months for container query parent support.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Tailwind v4 supports arbitrary values for ALL CSS features - @container (min-width: 400px) becomes @[400px]:flex immediately. And utility-first authoring is real velocity - I prototype components 2x faster in Tailwind.
debate.evidence: Internal poll of our 5 DS engineers: 4 prefer Tailwind for prototyping, 1 prefers CSS Modules for cleanup.

where_they_agree: Runtime tenant theming via CSS custom properties is required
DS ships compiled CSS, not source classes
Build-time consistency across 60 engineers matters
Tailwind v4's @theme is a real improvement over v3
where_they_disagree: Whether utility-first authoring is worth the build dependency
How much flexibility consumers actually need outside the 12 color tokens
Whether 'native CSS first' is a winning bet for a 3-year design system
context_dependent_answer: Choose Tailwind v4 when: DS team prefers utility-first, consumers willing to standardize on tooling, theming bounded to colors + density modes, prototyping velocity matters. Choose CSS Modules when: consumers vary widely in toolchain, you ship to web components or non-React environments, team prioritizes native CSS feature adoption, per-tenant theming extends beyond colors.

strengths: Tokens are pure CSS vars - infinite flexibility
weaknesses: Type-safe class names need extra tooling

decision_framework: Ask: (1) DS team prefers utility-first or CSS-first authoring? Survey them. (2) Consumers willing to standardize toolchain? Yes -> either; no -> compiled CSS shipping. (3) Theming bounded to tokens or open-ended? Bounded -> Tailwind; open-ended -> CSS Modules. (4) Using bleeding-edge native CSS (anchor positioning, view transitions)? Yes -> lean CSS Modules.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors treat this as 'Tailwind vs CSS' as a religious war. Seniors recognize it's a decision about authoring DX for the DS team and distribution model for consumers - the runtime output (CSS with custom properties) can be identical either way. They also know v4's @theme directive fundamentally changed Tailwind's runtime story; arguments based on v3 limitations don't apply.

scenario: A travel booking site needs smooth shared-element transitions when a user clicks a hotel card and it expands into a detail view. Choices: Framer Motion's layoutId, the native View Transitions API, or a custom FLIP implementation. Performance on mid-range Android is a hard requirement.
context: Team of 7, Next.js 15 App Router. Currently uses Framer Motion. Lighthouse mobile is monitored. View Transitions are Baseline (Chrome 111+, Safari 18, Firefox 135+).
alex_initial_position: Native View Transitions API. It's GPU-composited by the browser, costs zero JS at runtime, and now Baseline. Framer Motion's layoutId triggers layout recalculations every frame - expensive on Android.
sam_initial_position: Framer Motion. View Transitions work for page navigations but the API for cross-fade between specific elements is awkward (view-transition-name juggling, can't handle list animations). Framer's layoutId is one prop; the DX delta is huge.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: I measured this. Framer's layoutId runs measure -> animate via transform, but during the animation it triggers React state updates that re-render the destination. On a Pixel 6a, the hotel card expansion took 380ms with 12 dropped frames.
debate.evidence: Chrome DevTools trace from staging: long task at 280ms in framer-motion bundle during expansion.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That trace shows the destination view fetching and rendering, not the animation itself. Framer's layout animation is transform-based and runs on the compositor. You're attributing data-fetch jank to the animation library.
debate.evidence: Framer Motion source: useLayoutAnimation uses requestAnimationFrame + transform translate/scale, GPU-composited.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK partial concession. But Framer Motion is 60KB gzipped just for layout animations. View Transitions API is built in - zero KB. On a hotel listing where we want fast TTI, that's meaningful.
debate.evidence: bundlephobia: framer-motion v11 is 60.2KB gzipped. View Transitions API is 0 bytes.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 60KB amortized across 12 animations is 5KB per animation. We're already shipping it for modals. Marginal cost is zero. View Transitions also require document.startViewTransition which only works for full DOM swaps - our hotel card stays mounted and morphs.
debate.evidence: View Transitions API spec: 'The old DOM state is captured as snapshot, the new state replaces it.' Our element stays in DOM.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That IS the model. We don't keep the card mounted - we navigate to /hotel/[id] which renders the detail view with the same view-transition-name. Browser handles the morph. This is the canonical use case.
debate.evidence: Chrome's View Transitions sample app does this exact pattern for an Airbnb-style card-to-detail transition.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That only works if we route to a new URL. What about the in-page modal expansion? PMs want both - some hotels open modal, some open full route. View Transitions can't do the modal case smoothly without manual snapshot tricks.
debate.evidence: MDN: 'document.startViewTransition is intended for cases where you're changing the DOM in a way that wouldn't normally animate.' Mid-page expansion is awkward.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Fair. We hybrid: View Transitions for route-level (card -> detail page), Framer or CSS for in-modal. Big-cost transitions use the free API; in-place stays in our existing stack.
debate.evidence: Astro's animations docs recommend exactly this split: View Transitions for navigation, CSS or library for in-page.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Acceptable, but we wrap View Transitions in a thin abstraction so component code doesn't change between modes. Otherwise we'll have two animation styles and engineers won't know which one applies.
debate.evidence: Linear's eng blog on animation systems: 'Uniform component API regardless of underlying primitive prevents drift.'

where_they_agree: Mid-range Android performance decides
Bundle size matters but isn't only factor
Route navigation transitions should use View Transitions API
A wrapper abstraction prevents engineer confusion
where_they_disagree: Whether Framer Motion's runtime cost is actually significant
How to handle in-place vs route animations consistently
Whether View Transitions API is mature enough as primary
context_dependent_answer: Use View Transitions API when: animations tied to route changes, you control both old and new DOM, you can establish unique view-transition-name on matched elements. Use Framer Motion when: animations are gestural (drag, hover physics), elements stay mounted and morph in place, you need orchestration (staggered children, AnimatePresence exits). Hybrid is typical: View Transitions for navigation, Framer for in-place/gestural.
decision_framework: For each animation ask: (1) Tied to a route change? Yes -> View Transitions. (2) Element stays mounted and morphs in place? Yes -> Framer or CSS. (3) Gestural (drag, spring physics)? Yes -> Framer. (4) On a critical performance budget? Yes -> prefer native. (5) Engineers learning animation APIs ad-hoc? Yes -> wrap in component abstraction.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors reach for Framer because the API is familiar and demos look great. Seniors profile on real mid-range hardware (not their MacBooks) and discover layoutId animations can drop frames during React commit phases. They also recognize that View Transitions API isn't a replacement for Framer - they solve different problems (cross-DOM-state vs in-place state) and a mature app uses both.

scenario: A fintech checkout flow team is debating: Mock Service Worker (MSW) for all integration tests vs. in-component mocking via jest.mock at the module boundary. The checkout has 14 components touching 8 different API endpoints with complex error states (3DS challenges, rate limits, expired sessions).
context: Team of 9, Vitest + RTL. Test suite at 1200 tests, 90s run time. Engineers complain tests are fragile when API shapes change. PCI scope means we need high coverage on error paths.
alex_initial_position: jest.mock at module boundaries. MSW adds a whole network mocking layer, slower test startup, and you end up debugging worker registration issues. Module mocks are explicit, fast, and the failure modes are obvious.
sam_initial_position: MSW everywhere. We mock once at the HTTP layer and every test gets realistic API behavior - including error codes, headers, and timing. Module mocks tie tests to implementation details; rename your API client and 200 tests break.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Our last big refactor moved from axios to fetch. With module mocks, 340 tests broke - each was mocking the axios instance. With MSW, those would have kept working because they intercept at the network layer, not the function call.
debate.evidence: Git history: PR #4521 'Migrate to fetch' touched 340 test files, mostly mock setup.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's a one-time cost vs. ongoing cost. MSW startup adds 800ms per test file because the worker has to initialize, and you pay it whether the test makes 0 or 50 requests. We have 200 test files - that's 160s of pure overhead.
debate.evidence: Vitest profiling output: setupFiles hook averages 820ms when MSW is enabled, 12ms without.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That overhead is per-worker in node mode, amortized across all tests in the file. With pool: 'threads' and isolated: false, MSW initializes once per worker. Measured cost is closer to 30s total in a 4-thread run.
debate.evidence: MSW 2.x docs on Node.js: 'setupServer() can be invoked once per worker thread and reused.'

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Fine on cost. But debuggability: when an MSW handler doesn't match a request, you get 'no handler for GET /api/cart' which doesn't tell you why. With jest.mock, the call expectation tells you exactly which function wasn't mocked.
debate.evidence: Last week: spent 2 hours finding that a test was hitting the real Stripe endpoint because MSW's URL pattern matcher treated query strings inconsistently.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's an MSW config issue, not a paradigm issue. With onUnhandledRequest: 'error' you get a hard failure with the exact URL. And MSW gives you something module mocks can't: testing the request body, headers, and the integration with React Query's caching.
debate.evidence: MSW docs: onUnhandledRequest option throws with full request details. Plus we can assert on retry-after headers in rate-limit tests.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: You can test request bodies with jest.mock too - mock.calls[0][0]. The real difference is: MSW couples your tests to the network layer, which means if you switch from REST to tRPC or GraphQL, MSW handlers need rewriting. Module mocks abstract over transport.
debate.evidence: We're piloting tRPC for the new admin app. MSW would need a separate handler setup; module mocks of the trpc client work the same.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: tRPC has @trpc/server/adapters/node-http and MSW can intercept those calls. But more importantly: testing what your code does in response to actual HTTP responses (with status codes, headers, body parsing) catches bugs module mocks miss. We had a bug where 422 was treated like 500 - module mocks never caught it because the mock returned 'throw new Error'.
debate.evidence: Bug ID PAY-892: fixed status code handling caught only in QA, would have been caught by MSW's realistic responses.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK conceded - MSW catches HTTP-layer bugs better. Compromise: MSW for the 14 checkout integration tests where HTTP behavior matters; module mocks for the other 1180 unit tests that just need a stub return value.
debate.evidence: Pyramid principle: integration tests warrant realistic mocking; unit tests warrant fast isolated mocking.

where_they_agree: Test maintenance cost is the real constraint
HTTP status code behavior must be tested somewhere
Implementation-detail coupling in tests is a smell
Test pyramid - different mock strategies at different levels
where_they_disagree: Whether MSW's runtime cost is acceptable
Whether HTTP-layer testing is worth the setup complexity
How to handle the transport-change refactor scenario
context_dependent_answer: Use MSW when: testing integration flows (checkout, signup), the test must assert HTTP details (status, headers, retries), the team is on a stable transport (REST or GraphQL committed). Use module mocks when: testing isolated component logic, the function-under-test calls a typed client (not raw fetch), test startup time is critical. Hybrid: MSW for integration tier, module mocks for unit tier - typically aligned with file naming (.integration.test.ts vs .test.ts).

weaknesses: Debugging unhandled requests can be tricky
Couples tests to client function shape
Refactor of transport breaks many tests

decision_framework: Test type drives mock strategy: (1) Integration tests covering a feature flow -> MSW. (2) Unit tests for a single component's logic -> module mocks. (3) E2E tests -> real backend or contract-based stub. Apply consistently via file naming convention so engineers know which they're writing.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick one mocking strategy and use it everywhere, optimizing for consistency over fit. Seniors recognize that the test pyramid maps to mock strategy: realistic mocks (MSW) for fewer integration tests, fast isolated mocks (module) for many unit tests. They also instrument test startup and per-test timing to catch when mock setup becomes the bottleneck.

scenario: A backend-for-frontend team is adopting discriminated unions to model UI state. Specifically, an async data widget can be in states: idle, loading, success(data), error(message). Debate: model with a discriminated union vs. multiple boolean flags + optional data fields.
context: Team of 8, TypeScript strict + noUncheckedIndexedAccess. Codebase has 40+ widgets with subtly different state machines. Junior engineer wrote a widget that displayed stale data while loading=true and error=true simultaneously.
alex_initial_position: Discriminated unions are over-applied. They look elegant but force ceremony on every consumer - exhaustive switches, narrowing dance. For widgets that genuinely have 4 mutually exclusive states it's fine, but most widgets want 'show old data while refreshing' which DUs make harder.
sam_initial_position: Discriminated unions are non-negotiable for state with mutually exclusive variants. The bug we just had - simultaneously loading and error - is impossible to express with a DU. The compiler enforces what code reviews can't.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: With { isLoading: boolean; isError: boolean; data?: T; error?: string } you can construct { isLoading: true, isError: true, data: oldData, error: 'failed' } - which is exactly the bug we shipped. TypeScript happily accepts it.
debate.evidence: PR #5612 bug report: the impossible state was constructed in a useEffect that didn't clear the previous error before starting a new fetch.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: You can fix that with a reducer that enforces transitions, no DU required. And the cost of DUs: every consumer needs `if (state.status === 'success') { state.data }` - that's 4 extra characters and a switch fallthrough story on every read.
debate.evidence: Internal codebase analysis: switching to DUs added 1200 lines of narrowing code across the existing widgets.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 4 extra characters vs. compile-time impossibility. The 1200 lines are mostly switch statements that ALSO serve as documentation - a new engineer reads 'switch (state.status)' and instantly knows what variants exist. Boolean flags require chasing all setState calls to learn the state machine.
debate.evidence: Reading test: showed both forms to 5 engineers, asked 'what are the possible states?' DU read in 8s avg, boolean flags in 45s avg.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Documentation argument is fair. But 'show stale data while refetching' - React Query's exact UX pattern - doesn't fit a strict DU. You'd need 'refetching with stale data' as its own variant, which means consumers handle that variant separately from 'success'.
debate.evidence: React Query exposes data + isFetching + isError as orthogonal fields precisely because the states aren't mutually exclusive.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's an argument for separating 'data' from 'status'. Model it as { status: 'idle' | 'loading' | 'success' | 'error'; data?: T; lastError?: string } where status describes the current operation and data is the cache. DU for the operation, plain field for the cache.
debate.evidence: This is exactly XState's pattern: discriminated state for the FSM, orthogonal 'context' for cached values.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK that's a clean model. But you've conceded that 'pure DU' isn't right - you need DU + orthogonal fields. So the answer is 'use DUs where states are truly mutually exclusive, not as a religion.' Which is my position.
debate.evidence: My original position: 'For widgets that genuinely have 4 mutually exclusive states, DU is fine.'

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: I'll take that compromise. Rule: model the operation as a DU (idle/loading/success/error), but data and error message can live as orthogonal fields when they need to outlive the variant transition. Booleans for state are banned outright.
debate.evidence: Banning booleans removes the entire class of impossible states from the type system.

where_they_agree: Multiple boolean flags allow impossible states and are a smell
Discriminated unions document state machines naturally
'Show stale data while refreshing' is a legitimate pattern
Operation status and cached data are different concerns
where_they_disagree: Whether DUs should be the default for all async state
How much narrowing ceremony is acceptable
Whether to model React Query's pattern with a strict DU
context_dependent_answer: Use a discriminated union when: states are genuinely mutually exclusive (modal open/closed/loading-open), the state machine has 3+ variants you need to document, the type can't fix the impossible-state bug otherwise. Use orthogonal fields (or DU + orthogonal data) when: you need to cache values across state transitions (stale data while refetching), the underlying library models it that way (React Query), the variant count is 2 and binary.

code_examples.approach: Discriminated union for operation + orthogonal data
strengths: Operation status documents the state machine
weaknesses: More verbose than booleans for simple cases

strengths: Each variant has only the fields it needs
weaknesses: Can't show stale data during refetch
Doesn't match React Query's actual model

decision_framework: Ask: (1) Are the states genuinely mutually exclusive at the moment of observation? Yes -> DU. (2) Do you need to preserve a value (data, message) across transitions? Yes -> DU + orthogonal field. (3) Is the consumer code reading this state in 10+ places? Yes -> DU regardless of cost. (4) Is this a 2-state binary (on/off, expanded/collapsed)? Boolean is fine.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors either adopt DUs as a religion (and lose stale-data UX) or stay on booleans (and ship impossible-state bugs). Seniors recognize that 'state' is two things: the operation lifecycle (DU) and the cached values (fields), and model them separately. They also know that the cost of narrowing is bounded and one-time, while the cost of impossible-state bugs compounds over the project's lifetime.

scenario: A note-taking app implements optimistic UI for: creating a note, renaming, deleting, moving between folders. Team debates commit-then-revert (apply locally immediately, undo on failure) vs. deferred (show pending indicator, only commit on server ack). Server has 200-800ms latency p50/p95.
context: Team of 6, React 19 with useOptimistic. Users on slow networks (Brazil, India) noticed lag. Some 'rename' operations have validation server-side (no duplicate names per folder).
alex_initial_position: Commit-then-revert with useOptimistic. Modern apps feel instant. The revert UX is 0.1% edge case - 99% of ops succeed and users get instant feedback for free. Deferred UX with spinners feels old.
sam_initial_position: Deferred with pending indicators for any operation with server-side validation. Commit-then-revert is fine for idempotent operations (toggle, like) but breaks horribly when validation rejects - the user types a name, sees it appear, then it snaps back. That's worse than a spinner.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Take rename. User types 'My Project', sees it appear, server says 'duplicate name'. We have to revert to the old name AND show an error. The user just lost the text they typed, plus they're confused why the name changed back.
debate.evidence: User testing recording: 8/10 users described the revert experience as 'a glitch' rather than understanding it as an error.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's a UX implementation problem, not an optimistic UI problem. Show the new name with a subtle pending state, keep the input editable while server validates. On error, the input stays with the typed value plus an inline error. We don't 'snap back' - we resolve forward.
debate.evidence: Linear's rename UX: optimistic display with a 'saving' dot, on error the value stays visible with red border and message.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's exactly deferred UI with a different visual treatment. You said 'subtle pending state, keep the input editable'. That IS pending indicator. The disagreement is just whether the value is shown as 'this is the value' vs 'this is being saved'.
debate.evidence: Your description matches the 'deferred with optimistic value display' pattern from Nielsen Norman Group's optimistic UI taxonomy.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Fair, terminology overlap. My real point: useOptimistic in React 19 makes this trivial - one line for the optimistic value, automatic revert on async failure. Implementing deferred with explicit pending states means a useState for value + useState for pending + manual revert logic.
debate.evidence: Code comparison: useOptimistic version is 12 lines, manual pending state version is 38 lines for the same UX.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: useOptimistic only reverts when the surrounding action throws. If the server returns 422 with an error body, you have to throw inside the action to trigger revert - which means your error handler can't use the response. That's a leaky abstraction.
debate.evidence: React 19 docs: 'useOptimistic state resets when the async action returns or throws.' To revert with error info, you must throw, which discards the typed return type.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: You catch the response, build an error object, then throw it. Type the thrown value via a custom Error class. Or: useOptimistic + a separate useState for the persistent error message. Two pieces of state, but still simpler than manual deferred.
debate.evidence: react-hook-form does exactly this - throws a typed FormError to trigger reset, with errors stored separately.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: OK conceded for rename. But take 'move note to folder'. User drags note from Folder A to Folder B. Optimistic: note disappears from A, appears in B. Server validates and rejects (e.g. archived folder). Now the note has to teleport back. That visual disruption is much worse than a brief 'moving...' state.
debate.evidence: User study: drag-and-drop with revert had 22% higher 'this feels broken' rating vs. drag with pending state.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Drag-drop is its own category - the gesture itself is the commitment, so the visual must reflect immediately or the gesture feels broken. But for that case use optimistic locally with a 'soft' pending opacity, and only revert if rejection happens within the first 500ms. After that, treat as accepted and show error inline.
debate.evidence: Figma's drag-to-move uses exactly this pattern: optimistic with a 500ms 'soft commit' window.

where_they_agree: Server-side validation requires special UX consideration
Pure commit-then-revert is bad for non-idempotent operations
useOptimistic works for simple cases
Drag-drop gestures need optimistic visuals
where_they_disagree: Whether 'optimistic with pending dot' is optimistic or deferred
How to surface errors after optimistic commit
Whether useOptimistic is sufficient or needs supplementing
context_dependent_answer: Use commit-then-revert (useOptimistic alone) when: the operation is idempotent (toggle, like, mark-read), validation is client-side only, the revert visual cost is acceptable. Use deferred with optimistic value display when: validation is server-side (rename with uniqueness, move with permissions), the user is actively engaged with the affected element. Use hybrid (optimistic + 500ms soft-commit) when: the operation is gestural (drag-drop, swipe), or the user expects immediate feedback.

strengths: Error state separated from optimistic value
weaknesses: Throwing to trigger revert is awkward
Two pieces of state to coordinate
Disabled input during save can frustrate users

decision_framework: Per operation ask: (1) Is the operation idempotent and unlikely to fail validation? Optimistic. (2) Does the operation have server-side validation that can reject? Optimistic-display-with-pending or deferred. (3) Is the operation gestural (drag, swipe)? Optimistic with soft-commit window. (4) Will revert cause visual whiplash (large content shift)? Avoid pure optimistic.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors apply optimistic UI uniformly because 'modern apps feel instant'. Seniors recognize that the right pattern depends on validation surface and operation type, and that the 'revert visual' has a real UX cost when it happens. They also know useOptimistic's revert-on-throw is an opinionated primitive that doesn't fit every case, and supplement it with explicit error state when needed.

scenario: A B2B SaaS company is choosing how to ship reusable form components across 5 product teams. Options: (a) a published npm package with versioned releases, (b) a Nx workspace with the library as an internal package, (c) a 'copy-paste from a registry' approach like shadcn-ui where each team owns their copy.
context: 5 product teams, ~40 engineers total. Different release cadences. A previous npm library effort had version-skew bloat - 4 different versions running in prod. Design team wants more rapid iteration.
alex_initial_position: Nx monorepo with internal package. We avoid version skew (everyone is on HEAD), we get atomic refactors when a component API changes, and CI catches breakages immediately. npm releases are 1990s thinking for an internal library.
sam_initial_position: shadcn-style copy-paste registry. The npm package model couples teams to a release cycle they didn't choose. Copy-paste gives each team a customizable starting point. Version skew is a feature, not a bug - teams adopt at their pace.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Copy-paste guarantees drift. Six months from now we have 5 different versions of Button with subtly different accessibility behaviors, none of them fully right. The whole point of a library is forcing consistency.
debate.evidence: shadcn-ui itself has known a11y inconsistencies because teams modify the copies. GitHub issues full of 'we patched X locally.'

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That's a governance problem solved by a 'core' set in the registry that teams can update via CLI. shadcn's `npx shadcn-ui add button` overwrites the local copy. Drift only happens if teams refuse to update, which is a culture issue not an architecture issue.
debate.evidence: Vercel's internal Geist registry uses exactly this pattern - core components are CLI-updated, teams have local-only overrides in named slots.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Culture is harder to enforce than CI. With Nx, a PR that breaks Button's API fails CI in all 5 apps simultaneously. With copy-paste, the breaking change exists in one team's copy and propagates by goodwill. We had 4 versions in prod because of this.
debate.evidence: Last year's compliance audit found 4 versions of our DateInput component in production with 3 different validation behaviors.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: 4 versions because npm released them and teams didn't update. With copy-paste, there ARE no versions - just snapshots. The conformance check is automated: a script verifies each team's copy matches the registry hash, or lists their intentional customizations.
debate.evidence: Stripe's internal design tokens repo uses a 'drift report' CI job that flags divergences without blocking - 95% conformance rate without forcing updates.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's interesting but assumes teams will accept drift reports as binding. In our culture, soft signals get ignored. Hard CI failures don't. Nx's enforced version is the cultural intervention we need.
debate.evidence: Compare: design tokens linter (soft) - 30% adoption. ESLint --max-warnings 0 in CI (hard) - 100% adoption.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: OK, but Nx introduces a new problem: the library team becomes a bottleneck. Every breaking change needs coordination across 5 apps. Currently when we wanted to add a new variant to Button, we needed sign-off from all 5 product teams. That took 3 weeks.
debate.evidence: Internal PR #3892 'Add destructive Button variant' - opened 2024-08-12, merged 2024-09-04, 22 days.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's a workflow problem - additive changes shouldn't require sign-off. New variant is non-breaking, ship it. The 3-week delay was because we treated additions like breaking changes. Fix the process, not the architecture.
debate.evidence: Semver: minor versions are additive. Our PR review template was the bottleneck, not Nx.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Real concession: with proper additive-vs-breaking discipline, Nx works. Let me propose a hybrid: Nx internal package for primitives (Button, Input, Field, etc.), shadcn-style registry for application-level compositions (LoginForm, BillingForm, etc.) where teams genuinely need to customize.
debate.evidence: Atomic design distinction: atoms in shared package, organisms in registry.

where_they_agree: Version skew in prod is real and harmful
Component library effort needs governance, not just code
Additive changes shouldn't require coordination
Hybrid models can address different component types
where_they_disagree: Whether cultural enforcement (drift reports) can match CI enforcement
Where the library team's coordination cost lives
Whether copy-paste's flexibility is worth its consistency risk
context_dependent_answer: Use Nx workspace with internal package when: teams share a release cadence, breaking changes are rare, you have strong central design team ownership, atoms are the dominant share. Use shadcn-style registry when: teams have divergent customization needs, the components are application-specific (forms, layouts), the design team values team-level autonomy. Hybrid (most common at scale): Nx for primitives, registry for compositions.

weaknesses: All apps must build the workspace

strengths: Teams own their copies and customize freely

decision_framework: Ask: (1) Do teams share a release cadence? Yes -> Nx. (2) Is the central design team large enough to coordinate? Yes -> Nx. (3) Are component customizations primarily compositional (app-specific layouts)? Yes -> registry. (4) Have you had version skew problems? Yes -> Nx for affected layer. (5) Are teams politically autonomous (acquisitions, BU split)? Yes -> registry, possibly with shared primitives.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors evaluate this as 'npm vs not-npm'. Seniors recognize the deeper question: where does coordination cost live? npm/Nx centralizes it (library team), registries distribute it (each app team). The answer depends on which org structure matches the company's reality. They also know that 'we'll just enforce it culturally' fails at scale - hard signals (CI) beat soft signals (drift reports) when stakes are real.

scenario: A design system team is debating the public API for a Button component: a single variants object (cva-style with intent/size/loading/etc.) versus boolean props (isPrimary, isLarge, isLoading). The team has 35 consumers and adds new variants every quarter.
context: Team of 4 on DS, TS strict. Two seniors built the original boolean API. Current Button has 14 boolean props; some combinations are nonsensical (isPrimary + isGhost). DX feedback is mixed.
alex_initial_position: Variants object via cva. Booleans allow impossible combinations (isPrimary && isSecondary), don't compose well in TS, and produce horrible autocomplete with 14 unrelated props. cva gives one place for the type, one place for the styles.
sam_initial_position: Keep boolean props for the common cases. `<Button isPrimary isLoading>` reads beautifully and reflects how engineers think. cva's `<Button intent='primary' state='loading'>` adds a layer of indirection that's worse DX for the 80% case.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: With 14 booleans, autocomplete on `<Button` shows isPrimary, isSecondary, isGhost, isDestructive, isOutline... all 14 props as if they're independent. But they're mutually exclusive. The type system doesn't know that. cva's `intent: 'primary' | 'secondary' | ...` is the actual model.
debate.evidence: VS Code IntelliSense recording: typing `<Button is` shows 14 suggestions ordered alphabetically. Users had to scroll to find the right one.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You can solve that with a discriminated union of boolean props - { isPrimary: true } | { isSecondary: true } | etc. Get the 'one true variant' guarantee with the natural-feeling syntax.
debate.evidence: Radix's some components use this exact pattern - mutually exclusive boolean props in a DU.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That DU explodes with size and state. intent (4) * size (3) * state (3) = 36 variants. A DU of 36 object literals is incomprehensible. cva separates the dimensions: each is independent, the product is implicit.
debate.evidence: Try writing the union manually: Pick<{isPrimary, isSmall, isLoading}, ...> - it's 36 lines. cva config is 9 lines.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: But what's the actual user pain? Engineers write `<Button isPrimary>` 90% of the time. They don't write the 36-variant matrix. The component API should optimize for the common case, not the spec.
debate.evidence: Internal code analysis: 87% of Button usages set exactly 0 or 1 intent prop. Only 2% combine intent + size + state.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's an argument for sensible defaults, not for boolean props. With cva: `<Button>` is primary medium idle. `<Button intent='destructive'>` overrides one dimension. Same brevity, type-safe combination.
debate.evidence: shadcn-ui Button default values: intent='default', size='default'. Most usage is `<Button>text</Button>` with no props.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Fine on intent. What about state-like booleans: isLoading, isDisabled? Those don't compose with intent - they're orthogonal. `<Button isLoading>` reads better than `<Button state='loading'>` because 'loading' isn't really a variant of style, it's a behavior modifier.
debate.evidence: HTML's own design: <input disabled> not <input state='disabled'>. Boolean attributes for behavioral modifiers are idiomatic.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Strong point. Behavioral modifiers (disabled, loading) ARE different from visual variants (intent, size). Compromise: cva for visual variants (intent, size, shape), boolean props for behavioral modifiers (isLoading, isFullWidth). Mixed but principled.
debate.evidence: Tailwind's Headless UI follows this split exactly: as / size as variants, disabled / open as booleans.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. Rule: if it's a stylistic 'kind', use a variant string. If it's a behavioral modifier, use a boolean. The 14-boolean original was wrong because it conflated kinds with modifiers.
debate.evidence: Our pain wasn't booleans - it was booleans abused for variants. The cva-only approach swings the other way.

where_they_agree: Original 14-boolean API is broken
Type system should prevent impossible combinations
Common case (no props) should be ergonomic
Mixing approaches is acceptable when principled
where_they_disagree: Whether boolean DU can scale to 36 variants
How much to weight 'reads naturally' vs type-safety
Where to draw the line between variants and behavioral modifiers
context_dependent_answer: Use variants object (cva) when: the prop is a visual 'kind' with mutually exclusive values (intent, size, shape, radius), the dimension has 3+ values. Use boolean props when: the prop is a behavioral modifier (disabled, loading, fullWidth, autoFocus), the prop is a binary on/off, the prop is independent of all other props.

code_examples.approach: cva variants for kinds + booleans for behavior
strengths: Variants are mutually exclusive by type
Sensible defaults make common case zero-prop
Each prop reads naturally in isolation
weaknesses: Internal mapping to actual variant is awkward
No type-system support for mutual exclusion

decision_framework: For each prop ask: (1) Is this a stylistic 'kind' with mutually exclusive options? -> variant string in cva. (2) Is this a behavioral on/off modifier independent of kinds? -> boolean. (3) Does this prop's value affect 2+ other props' valid values? -> variant string with compound variants in cva. (4) Is this a native HTML attribute behavior (disabled, required, autoFocus)? -> boolean to match HTML idioms.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors either flatten everything to booleans (familiar but type-unsafe) or push everything into variants (type-safe but awkward for behavioral modifiers). Seniors split the API along the kind-vs-modifier axis: variants where mutual exclusion matters, booleans where independence and HTML idioms matter. They also know that 'natural reads' is a real DX concern but not the only one - autocomplete quality, type safety, and refactor-ability all weigh in.

scenario: A multi-step KYC (know-your-customer) flow with 6 steps, conditional fields (US users see SSN, EU users see VAT ID), file uploads with progress, and server-side validation that returns field-level errors. Debate: react-hook-form + zod vs. TanStack Form vs. a custom useReducer-based solution.
context: Team of 7, React 19. RHF is in the rest of the app but starts to creak on multi-step flows. TanStack Form is new but native to TanStack ecosystem already in use.
alex_initial_position: TanStack Form. It's actually-typed end-to-end (RHF's generic inference is fragile), supports async validation natively, and integrates with TanStack Router for step state. RHF's resolver pattern was a great hack but not how I'd build it today.
sam_initial_position: react-hook-form + zod. The ecosystem maturity matters - every developer knows it, devtools work, every form pattern has a Stack Overflow answer. TanStack Form's APIs aren't stable enough to bet a critical onboarding flow on.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: This is a flow that affects revenue. KYC failures lose customers. We need a library where every edge case (re-validation on blur, conditional fields, file inputs, server errors) has been seen 1000 times. RHF has that battle-testing.
debate.evidence: npm: react-hook-form 9M weekly downloads vs TanStack Form 80k. Stack Overflow: 8.5k RHF questions vs 200 TanStack Form.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Battle-testing matters for stable APIs. But RHF's typing breaks every time we add a conditional field. We had to use `as any` 3 times last month because Path<TFieldValues> doesn't infer through discriminated unions. TanStack Form gets this right.
debate.evidence: GitHub issue react-hook-form#11234 'Conditional field types break with DU' - open for 8 months, marked as 'tracking'.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You can split the form by step - each step is its own RHF form with its own schema. Conditional fields become 'conditional step' which composes naturally. No DU-on-FieldValues complexity.
debate.evidence: Stripe's checkout form does this - one RHF instance per logical step, parent component coordinates.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That breaks cross-step validation and breadcrumb 'go back to step 2' state. If each step is its own RHF, going back loses the values unless we lift state. At which point we're managing state outside RHF and the abstraction is leaking.
debate.evidence: react-hook-form FAQ: 'For multi-step forms, lift values to a parent state.' That's literally moving outside the library.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: `react-hook-form` has `<FormProvider>` + named forms in v7.55+. You can have one root form, separate components per step that share the underlying form instance. Values persist across steps automatically.
debate.evidence: RHF docs section 'Wizard / Multi-step forms': useFormContext() in each step component reads from a shared parent <FormProvider>.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That works but only when all fields can be in one schema. With our KYC, US users have `ssn` and EU users have `vatId` - they shouldn't both be in the union type or both validation rules run. TanStack Form handles this via per-step schemas with proper typing.
debate.evidence: TanStack Form's `validators` API allows per-step async validators that compose by region, with discriminated typing.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: zod handles that with `z.discriminatedUnion('region', [usSchema, euSchema])`. RHF's resolver passes the typed result through. We have this pattern in our billing form today.
debate.evidence: zod docs: discriminatedUnion with typed branches - RHF resolves to `region: 'us' ? { ssn: string } : { vatId: string }`.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK conceded zod handles the schema. My remaining concern is performance: RHF re-renders the form on every change unless you use Controller+watch carefully. TanStack Form's signal-based reactivity is per-field by default. On a 30-field KYC, that's measurable.
debate.evidence: Profiled our existing billing form: each keystroke triggers ~12ms reconciliation. TanStack Form equivalent: 2ms.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: RHF uses uncontrolled inputs by default, so most fields don't re-render at all on change. The 12ms is from Controller-wrapped fields. Use register() instead and you get TanStack-comparable perf.
debate.evidence: RHF benchmark page: register() avg 0.8ms per change, Controller avg 4ms per change. Mostly UI library coupling determines this.

where_they_agree: KYC is high-stakes and needs reliability
Type safety through conditional fields matters
Multi-step flows require value persistence across steps
Performance must be measured, not assumed
where_they_disagree: Whether RHF's ecosystem maturity outweighs TanStack Form's design wins
How to handle discriminated-union field schemas
Whether TanStack Form is stable enough for production KYC
context_dependent_answer: Use react-hook-form + zod when: ecosystem maturity matters (large team, knowledge sharing, plugins), the form fits the discriminatedUnion pattern, performance is acceptable with register(). Use TanStack Form when: TypeScript inference correctness is non-negotiable, you're already in the TanStack ecosystem (Router, Query), the team can absorb API churn risk. Build custom (useReducer) when: the flow is highly bespoke and no library shape fits cleanly - rare and usually wrong.

strengths: Values persist across steps via FormProvider
weaknesses: Generic inference fragile in edge cases
Conditional field type narrowing is awkward
Performance requires care with Controller usage

decision_framework: Ask: (1) Do you already use TanStack Router/Query and value cohesion? Yes -> TanStack Form. (2) Does your team's collective experience favor RHF? Yes -> RHF (unless type inference is a major blocker). (3) Does the form have conditional fields that don't fit discriminatedUnion? Yes -> TanStack Form. (4) Is it a critical flow where library churn is unacceptable? Yes -> RHF.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors pick form libraries based on docs polish or stars. Seniors evaluate based on how the library handles the specific edge cases they'll hit: conditional fields, async validation, multi-step persistence, server error mapping. They also know that 'mature ecosystem' is real value (knowledge transfer, plugin availability) but not the only value - design correctness matters when the team will live with the choice for years.

scenario: A media company is choosing the bundler for a new public-facing site. Options: Vite (Rolldown-based as of 2026), Turbopack (stable in Next 16), or webpack 5 with Rspack as a drop-in. Site has 150 routes, 80 MDX pages, large image processing pipeline, and a strict CSP requirement.
context: Team of 11, current build is webpack 5 taking 90s for prod and 25s for dev startup. Existing eslint/loader/babel customizations. Marketing pushes weekly content via MDX.
alex_initial_position: Rspack as drop-in for webpack. We keep our 30+ custom plugins working, get a 10x speedup, zero migration risk. Vite and Turbopack mean rewriting our entire build config and webpack plugins.
sam_initial_position: Vite (Rolldown). The webpack model is dying. Vite has the ecosystem momentum, the dev experience is dramatically better than Rspack's webpack-emulation, and Rolldown gives us Rust-speed prod builds. Migrate now or pay twice later.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: We have 8 internal webpack plugins. One alone (the CSP-nonce injection for inline scripts) took 3 weeks to write and integrates with our SSR. Rspack runs it as-is. Vite means rewriting it as a Vite plugin, plus testing against the SSR.
debate.evidence: Our CSP plugin: 340 lines, depends on webpack's compilation hooks. Vite's plugin API uses Rollup hooks with different lifecycle semantics.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That CSP plugin is exactly the kind of thing Vite has in its ecosystem already - `vite-plugin-csp` handles nonce injection in 50 lines of config. Migrating means deleting our 340-line plugin and replacing with one config block.
debate.evidence: vite-plugin-csp README: 'Generates CSP headers and nonces for inline scripts, works with vite-plugin-react SSR.'

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Maybe. But our other 7 plugins? The MDX-with-frontmatter pipeline, the image responsive-set generator, the build-time content indexing for search? Each is a custom investment we'd write off. Rspack preserves all of it.
debate.evidence: Our package.json lists @scope/webpack-mdx-frontmatter, @scope/webpack-image-srcset, @scope/webpack-search-index. All in-house.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Vite's ecosystem has equivalents: @mdx-js/rollup, vite-imagetools, vite-plugin-pages. The migration is mechanical, not architectural. And Vite's dev server is HMR-instant for content writers - they'd save hours per week.
debate.evidence: Internal survey of content writers: 'Build takes too long' is the #2 frustration after 'preview is slow'.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Rspack also has fast HMR - it's Rust-based dev server, not webpack-dev-server. We get the perf without migration cost. The 'webpack is dying' framing ignores that Rspack IS the modern alternative for webpack users.
debate.evidence: Rspack benchmarks: dev server start 1.2s vs webpack 25s. HMR update 50ms vs webpack 800ms.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Rspack's HMR matches Vite's for our codebase. But Rspack's plugin compatibility is partial - they explicitly say 'most webpack plugins work, some don't.' Our weird ones might fall in the 'some don't' bucket. We'd find out during migration anyway.
debate.evidence: Rspack compatibility doc lists 200+ tested plugins with status. Our internal plugins aren't on the list - undefined behavior.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: True but Rspack lets us migrate INCREMENTALLY - swap the build, keep config, fix what breaks. Vite migration is all-or-nothing. If we're risk-managing this, Rspack's incremental path is safer.
debate.evidence: Rspack migration guide: 'In most cases, replace webpack with @rspack/cli in package.json and existing config works.' Vite has no equivalent.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Acceptable framing. Hybrid proposal: Rspack now for the immediate perf win and zero-risk migration. Track Vite/Rolldown maturity for 12 months and re-evaluate. If our team grows or build needs become more SPA-like, Vite becomes the right answer.
debate.evidence: This is exactly Shopify's 2024 path - migrated from webpack to Rspack mid-2024, evaluating Vite for new projects in 2026.

where_they_agree: Current 90s prod build is unacceptable
Modern Rust-based bundlers are the answer
Migration risk must be weighed against perf gain
Plugin ecosystem compatibility is the deciding factor
where_they_disagree: Whether incremental migration (Rspack) or full migration (Vite) is the right risk profile
How much value to weight on Vite's ecosystem momentum
Whether webpack's API is a sunk cost worth preserving
context_dependent_answer: Choose Rspack when: you have webpack plugins worth preserving, the team has limited bandwidth for migration, you want immediate perf gains with low risk. Choose Vite when: you're starting fresh or have few custom plugins, the team values DX over migration cost, you want to be on the dominant frontend ecosystem long-term. Choose Turbopack when: you're committed to Next.js as the framework and want first-party tooling.

weaknesses: Custom webpack plugins must be rewritten

decision_framework: Ask: (1) Do you have webpack plugins worth >2 person-weeks to rewrite? Yes -> Rspack. (2) Is your app SPA or content-heavy? Content -> Vite (better SSG story). (3) Are you on Next.js? Turbopack is in-tree. (4) What is the team's risk tolerance for migration? Low -> Rspack. High -> Vite. (5) Is this app expected to live 5+ years? Bias toward Vite's ecosystem momentum.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors evaluate bundlers on benchmarks alone. Seniors evaluate on the integration shape: plugin ecosystem, framework integration, SSR support, deployment target. They also know that 'speed' is rarely the deciding factor at scale - your custom build logic, your team's webpack knowledge, your deployment pipeline all matter more than 30% vs 50% speed deltas.

scenario: A trading platform's error handling: the team debates per-route error boundaries (one fallback per page) vs. nested boundaries inside complex pages (per-widget) vs. global error boundary with telemetry. A previous incident had a single chart error blank the whole page.
context: Team of 9, React 19, Sentry for telemetry. Trading hours mean any blanked page = real money lost. Some widgets are critical (positions, orders), some are supplementary (news, charts).
alex_initial_position: Granular per-widget boundaries with Sentry capture. Each widget independently fails. The cost is some duplication of boundary setup, paid back 100x on the first incident where a chart bug doesn't kill the order entry.
sam_initial_position: Tiered boundaries: page-level for the main shell, with explicit boundaries around CRITICAL widgets only. Wrapping every widget in a boundary creates a forest of try-catches and obscures the actual error topology.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: We had the chart incident. Single boundary on the page meant chart bug took out everything including order entry. With per-widget boundaries, chart shows error state, traders keep trading. That's literal money saved.
debate.evidence: Incident report 2024-11-12: chart parse error -> full page error fallback -> trader locked out for 3 minutes. Estimated impact: $42k in missed fills.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Per-widget is overkill. The chart and order entry are critical. The news widget, the help panel, the chat - failures there don't matter. Wrap the critical ones, leave the rest under the page boundary. That's the right granularity.
debate.evidence: Stripe Connect dashboard architecture: 3 boundary levels (global, panel, critical-widget) rather than per-component.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: How do you know which are critical? Today the chart is critical. Tomorrow PMs add 'P&L flashing red when underwater' to the news widget. Now news is critical. The classification is fragile and political.
debate.evidence: PRD from last quarter promoted the formerly-supplementary 'alerts' widget to a trading-critical feature when we added price alerts.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Then categorize by data-criticality at component definition: a `<CriticalWidget>` and `<AuxiliaryWidget>` wrapper. The wrapper choice IS the criticality declaration. Promoting a widget means changing the wrapper - explicit and reviewed.
debate.evidence: This is the pattern: criticality as a component decision, made by the team owning the widget.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That works but it's the same as per-widget boundaries just with a coarser wrapper. The wrapper IS a boundary. We agree on the mechanism, disagree on the naming. Per-widget boundaries with criticality reporting to Sentry is the same outcome.
debate.evidence: My original position: 'granular per-widget boundaries with Sentry capture.' The difference is taxonomy.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Not quite - in my model, a widget without an explicit wrapper falls back to the page boundary. That's a meaningful default: 'if you don't declare criticality, the page handles it.' In yours, every widget gets a boundary regardless.
debate.evidence: Default behavior matters: my approach makes 80% of widgets cheap (page-level), yours makes them all explicit.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK but my reasoning: in trading, the page-level default IS the dangerous case. Anything inside the trading view should isolate by default unless explicitly opted out. Reverse the default - 'opt out of widget boundary' for chrome elements like footer/header.
debate.evidence: Defaults shape behavior. In a system where blanked pages = money lost, defaults should err toward isolation.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Fair. For this domain, isolation-by-default is correct. For most apps (CMS, internal tools), page-level default is right. Domain decides. Trading: per-widget by default. Internal admin: page-level. The principle: defaults reflect criticality of the domain.
debate.evidence: OWASP application criticality guidance: error isolation defaults should match the impact-of-failure profile of the user activity.

where_they_agree: A single global boundary is wrong
Telemetry to Sentry is required on every boundary
Criticality is a real concept that drives boundary placement
Domain context matters for the default
where_they_disagree: Whether per-widget should be the default or the exception
How explicit the criticality declaration should be
Whether 'wrapper component' and 'boundary' are functionally identical
context_dependent_answer: Use per-widget boundaries as default when: the domain has high failure cost (trading, medical, real-time), individual widgets are independently valuable, team can absorb the wrapping ceremony. Use page-level default with explicit critical-widget wrappers when: the domain has lower stakes (CMS, marketing, admin), widgets are mostly auxiliary, you want defaults to reflect 'most widgets are not critical'.

weaknesses: Wrapper at every widget call site
Slight perf cost of multiple boundaries
Auxiliary widget errors kill the page
Promoting a widget to critical requires code change

decision_framework: Ask: (1) What's the cost of a blanked page (revenue, safety, trust)? High -> isolation-by-default. Low -> page-level default. (2) Are widgets independently valuable to the user? Yes -> per-widget. (3) Can the team maintain wrapper discipline? Yes -> per-widget. (4) Are criticality lines clear and stable? Stable -> tiered works. Fluid -> isolation-by-default with criticality metadata.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors either wrap nothing in error boundaries (and ship pages that blank on any bug) or wrap everything (and create boundary soup that obscures real errors).
